main_summary.summary: Linda Fuller Degelmann, interviewed at her home in Americus, Georgia on May 28 by Emilye Crosby of the Southern Oral History Program at UNC-Chapel Hill, recounts her trajectory from a segregated upbringing in Tuscaloosa, Alabama, through a Montgomery business marriage with Millard (Dean) Fuller and Morris Dees of Fuller & Dees Marketing, to the founding of Habitat for Humanity International. Born in Birmingham in 1941, she married Millard in 1959, attended Huntingdon College, and witnessed her husband and Dees pivot from peripheral Klan-adjacent political work (speaking for MacDonald Gallion, subscribing to The Fiery Cross) after watching Freedom Riders beaten at the Montgomery bus station. She describes hosting Selma to Montgomery March participants in 1965, a marriage crisis that led the Fullers to give away their wealth, Dr. Howard A. Durgan of Broadway United Church of Christ recruiting Millard to raise a Tougaloo College dormitory fund, the 1968 move to Koinonia Farm to work with Clarence Jordan, the partnership housing model that became Habitat, three years building 120 houses in Zaire (then Congo), and the eventual growth of Habitat to 100 countries.
key_themes: Partnership housing and Habitat for Humanity origins
Freedom Rides and Selma to Montgomery March witness
Death-penalty defense and Black hiring boycott in Americus
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview documents the institutional origin of Habitat for Humanity International from inside the Koinonia Farm Christian community in Sumter County, Georgia, and connects that origin directly to the civil rights organizing context of Americus and the Albany Movement. Linda Fuller Degelmann's first-person account of Millard Fuller and Morris Dees observing the Montgomery Freedom Rides beatings, hosting Selma-to-Montgomery marchers, and partnering with Clarence Jordan on partnership housing for Black families denied bank loans provides specific witness testimony to how mid-1960s Christian-faith-grounded white Southerners moved from Klan-adjacent political work to civil rights allyship.

The interview also records the 1971 Black-led economic boycott and integrated march in Americus, the Unadilla, Georgia confrontation with police over visiting a Black family, the integration attempt at First Baptist Church Americus with Collins McGee, and the threats and firebombing directed at Koinonia residents including Gussie Jackson and Ethel Dunning. As a primary source it links the post-Selma white-Southern faith community to the partnership-housing economic-development model that scaled internationally through Habitat for Humanity.

chapters.title: Tuscaloosa Childhood and Segregation
chapters.summary: Born in Birmingham in 1941, raised in Tuscaloosa where her parents ran Caldwell Electric Company. Recounts a Black employee never invited to her parents' annual picnic, attending segregated schools, and being raised in a university town with limited racial awareness until after her 1959 marriage.

chapters.title: Montgomery and the Freedom Rides
chapters.summary: After marriage in 1959, moved to Montgomery where Millard (Dean) Fuller and Morris Dees built Fuller & Dees Marketing. Describes their peripheral Klan-adjacent political work speaking for MacDonald Gallion and subscribing to The Fiery Cross, then their pivot after witnessing Freedom Riders beaten at the Montgomery bus station and the retaliatory office break-in with typewriters smashed.

chapters.title: Selma to Montgomery Marchers in Their Home
chapters.summary: Hosted Northern pastors as Selma to Montgomery March participants at their home in 1965, receiving nasty phone calls from neighbors after license plates were photographed. Recounts the marriage crisis that led them to leave Montgomery and give away their wealth.

chapters.title: New York, Tougaloo, and Recommitment
chapters.summary: Dr. Howard A. Durgan of Broadway United Church of Christ, on Tougaloo College's board, recruited Millard to raise money for a new Tougaloo dormitory. Family relocated to Montclair, New Jersey for two years. Describes the Huntingdon College experience, the climate at Glen Ridge and Montclair, and continued reflection on race.

chapters.title: Moving to Koinonia Farm
chapters.summary: Summer 1968 move to Koinonia Farm in Sumter County, Georgia to work with Clarence Jordan. Describes Koinonia at low ebb with few residents, the Atlanta meeting between Millard and Clarence that produced the Koinonia Partnership concept, and the partnership-housing model: no profit, no interest, simple cinder-block houses sold to Black families denied bank loans.

chapters.title: Africa and the Founding of Habitat
chapters.summary: Three-year family relocation to Zaire (then Congo) to test the partnership-housing model internationally, building 120 houses with a Lilly Foundation grant for concrete. Return to Americus and the founding of Habitat for Humanity International in a chicken-barn office at Koinonia, with growth eventually to 100 countries and over a million people housed.

chapters.title: Koinonia Daily Life and Town Hostility
chapters.summary: Describes Koinonia's simple labor-board housing, the apartment above Clarence and Florence Jordan's first structure, hippie-era volunteers arriving from the North, mother's despair at the modest kitchen, and the experience of being shunned in Americus grocery stores when residents learned she lived at Koinonia. Recounts the historical violence: Conrad Browne arrested walking down the street sharing peanuts with a Black resident.

chapters.title: Threats Against Koinonia Black Families
chapters.summary: Recounts the threats that drove most Black families away from Koinonia in the 1950s, including the firebombing of Gussie Jackson's house. Describes the Unadilla, Georgia incident where the Fullers were arrested for visiting a Black family supporting a son's effort to integrate the white high school, then chased by a van of men to their motel.

chapters.title: Koinonia Nonviolence and Schools
chapters.summary: Koinonia residents took turns sitting at the road at night to demonstrate they were not afraid as cars drove by shooting; bullet holes remained from a near-miss of one of the Jordan daughters. Children's schools transitioned from Plains High to the integrated Americus schools after return from Africa, with Faith Fuller in class with Amy Carter.

chapters.title: First Baptist Americus and Collins McGee
chapters.summary: Recounts the December 1965 visit to Americus where the Fullers, Clarence Jordan, Allen and Carol Henry, and Collins McGee attempted to attend a Christmas service at First Baptist Church Americus where Allen Henry's father was preaching. The fire-chief deacon demanded McGee leave; the integrated group walked out together. Clarence remarked on the church's outside floodlights versus its dark interior.
related_events: First Baptist Americus integration attempt December 1965

chapters.title: Americus Black Boycott and Death-Penalty Law
chapters.summary: Describes the 1971 Americus Black-led economic boycott and integrated march demanding hiring of Black police officers. Millard (Dean) Fuller spoke at a Black church and counseled the crowd; he then took death-penalty cases representing Black defendants facing all-white juries, working with Donald Hollowell of Atlanta. Recounts Ethel Dunning whose white employer cut off her water when she moved into a Koinonia partnership house, and her response: 'I baked those cakes... I had to show them that I was bigger than how I was being treated.'

chapters.title: Faith, Family, and the Fuller Center
chapters.summary: Reflects on family rejection from both her side and Millard's after they gave away their wealth; her sister in California was the exception. Discusses Morris Dees's parallel trajectory to the Southern Poverty Law Center starting with Alabama State Patrol integration. Closes with reflection on civil rights as a template for other movements, her children's involvement in Habitat campus chapters and the Fuller Center for Housing founded in 2005, and a son's work for Saudi Aramco built on international upbringing.

loc_video.caption: Linda Fuller Degelmann interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Americus, Georgia, 2013 May 28
